content:
**Title: Fed Holds Rates Steady in October Amid Signs of Cooling Inflation**

**October 29, 2025 — Washington, D.C.**

The Federal Reserve’s Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) voted Wednesday to keep the upper bound of the target federal funds range at 5.25%, extending its cautious pause for a fourth consecutive meeting. The decision, announced after the two-day policy meeting, comes as inflation shows continued signs of retreat and economic growth moderates.

“The Committee judged that the current stance of monetary policy remains appropriate,” Fed Chair Jerome Powell said during the post-meeting press conference. “While inflation has eased notably over the past year, we remain attentive to risks and will not hesitate to adjust policy as needed.”

The vote was unanimous, signaling broad agreement among policymakers to maintain a restrictive posture until further progress on inflation is evident. The Fed’s benchmark rate has now remained unchanged at 5.25% since July, after an aggressive series of hikes in 2022 and 2023.

Recent data showed the Consumer Price Index rising at a 2.6% annual pace in September, inching closer to the Fed’s 2% target. Labor markets have also softened, with job gains slowing and wage growth moderating.

Markets responded calmly to the decision, with the S&P 500 holding steady and yields on 10-year Treasuries dipping slightly. Most analysts expect the Fed to keep rates elevated into early 2026, barring a sharp economic downturn.

“We’re in a wait-and-see mode,” said Alicia Zhang, chief U.S. economist at Greenleaf Capital. “The Fed is signaling patience, and so far, the data supports that stance.”
